# Complete Frontend Engineer Roadmap: Image Optimisation & Delivery

This is the complete learning path every Senior Frontend Engineer should understand for image performance.

***

# 1. Foundations: Why Image Optimisation Matters & How Browsers Load Images

## Why Images Matter

Images are often the largest assets on web pages and can significantly impact:

```text
✅ Page Speed
✅ LCP
✅ User Experience
✅ Mobile Performance
✅ SEO
✅ CDN Costs
```

Images are frequently among the heaviest resources on web pages, making optimisation a major performance opportunity.

***

## Browser Loading Lifecycle

```text
HTML Download
    ↓
HTML Parse
    ↓
DOM Creation
    ↓
Image Discovery
    ↓
Network Request
    ↓
Download
    ↓
Decode
    ↓
Paint
```

Images require both download and decoding before rendering.

***

# 2. Measuring Image Performance with Core Web Vitals

## Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)

Measures:

```text
Time until largest visible element appears
```

Good:

```text
< 2.5 seconds
```

The LCP element is often a hero image.

***

## Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)

Measures:

```text
Visual stability
```

Good:

```text
< 0.1
```

Images without dimensions commonly cause CLS problems.

***

## Interaction to Next Paint (INP)

Measures:

```text
Responsiveness after interaction
```

Good:

```text
< 200 ms
```

Heavy image processing and decoding can indirectly affect responsiveness.

***

## Monitoring Tools

```text
Chrome DevTools
Lighthouse
PageSpeed Insights
WebPageTest
RUM Platforms
```

Lighthouse and DevTools are commonly used to identify LCP elements and layout-shift contributors.

***

# 3. Reducing Image Bytes

## Resize Images

Bad:

```text
4000px image
displayed at 400px
```

Good:

```text
Serve the correct dimensions
```

Serving images at the appropriate display size reduces unnecessary transfer.

***

## Use Modern Formats

```text
JPEG
PNG
WebP
AVIF
```

WebP and AVIF generally provide more efficient compression than older formats.

***

## Compression

```text
Lossy
Lossless
```

Typical target:

```text
75–85% quality
```

Compression is one of the primary ways to reduce transferred image bytes.

***

## Metadata Removal

Remove:

```text
EXIF
GPS
Camera Information
```

to reduce image size.

***

# 4. Responsive Images: `srcset`, `sizes`, and `<picture>`

## `srcset`

Provides multiple image options:

photo.jpg![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Browser selects the most appropriate image for the device and viewport.

***

## `sizes`

Describes how much page space the image occupies:

(max-width: 600px) 100vw,800px![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

The browser combines this with device characteristics to choose an image candidate.

***

## `<picture>`

Used for:

```text
Format switching
Art direction
Different crops
```

Example:

&lt;picture&gt;&nbsp; mobile.webp&nbsp; desktop.jpg&lt;/picture&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

The `<picture>` element is specifically designed for format switching and art-direction scenarios.

***

# 5. Lazy Loading Images

## What Is Lazy Loading?

Images load only when approaching the viewport.

gallery.jpg![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

This reduces initial network activity and page weight.

***

## What To Lazy Load

```text
✅ Galleries
✅ Product grids
✅ Footer images
✅ Below-the-fold content
```

***

## What Not To Lazy Load

```text
❌ Hero image
❌ LCP image
❌ Above-the-fold image
```

Performance guidance consistently recommends avoiding lazy loading for images likely visible on initial viewport load.

***

# 6. Prioritising Critical Images

Critical images usually include:

```text
Hero Banner
Article Cover
Product Hero Image
```

These often become the LCP element.

***

## Fetch Priority

hero.webp![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

The Fetch Priority API allows developers to boost priority of important resources such as hero images.

***

## Preload Critical Images



Preloading starts discovery earlier than normal page parsing.

***

## Strategy

```text
Hero → High Priority
Above Fold → High Priority
Below Fold → Lazy Load
```

***

# 7. Image CDNs and Delivery Infrastructure

## Traditional Delivery

```text
User
 ↓
Origin Server
```

Problems:

```text
High latency
No optimisation
```

***

## Modern Image Delivery

```text
User
 ↓
Edge CDN
 ↓
Optimised Variant
```

Image CDNs combine global edge caching with image-specific optimisation and transformation capabilities.

***

## Capabilities

### Responsive Resizing

```text
width=400
width=1200
```

### Format Conversion

```text
AVIF
WebP
JPEG
```

### Compression

```text
Dynamic optimisation
```

### Edge Caching

Images are cached close to users around the world.

***

# 8. Production Monitoring and the Long Tail

## Lab Testing Is Not Enough

Local testing shows:

```text
Your machine
Your browser
Your network
```

Production involves:

```text
Thousands of devices
Different countries
Different ISPs
Different browsers
```

Real User Monitoring (RUM) captures this diversity.

***

## Use Percentiles

Avoid:

```text
Average
```

Prefer:

```text
P50
P75
P95
P99
```

Percentiles reveal long-tail performance issues hidden by averages.

***

## Why p75 Matters

Google reports Core Web Vitals using the **75th percentile**.

***

## Monitor

```text
LCP
CLS
INP
Image Load Times
Cache Hit Ratio
CDN Misses
```

Segment by:

```text
Country
Browser
Device
Network
```

RUM platforms commonly support these breakdowns.

***

# Senior Frontend Interview Summary

> Image optimisation is a full lifecycle concern. It starts with choosing the correct image dimensions, formats, and compression; continues through responsive delivery using `srcset`, `sizes`, and `<picture>`; improves loading through lazy loading and critical-image prioritisation; scales through Image CDNs and edge delivery; and is validated in production through Real User Monitoring and percentile-based Core Web Vitals analysis. The ultimate goal is to deliver the right image, in the right format, at the right time, to the right user.
